summary: Judea Pearl outlines the paradigmatic shift required to move from traditional statistical analysis to causal inference. He introduces the Structural Causal Model (SCM) as a unifying framework, emphasizing that causal conclusions require untestable causal assumptions and a specialized mathematical notation (like the do(x) operator and causal diagrams) distinct from standard probability calculus.

principles.statement: Behind every causal conclusion there must lie some causal assumption that is not testable in observational studies.
principles.rationale: The laws of probability theory do not dictate how a distribution changes when external conditions are modified. Therefore, causal claims cannot be substantiated from associations alone.
principles.source_quote: one cannot substantiate causal claims from associations alone, even at the population level—behind every causal conclusion there must lie some causal assumption that is not testable in observational studies.

principles.statement: Causal assumptions in path diagrams are encoded in the missing links, not the present ones.
principles.rationale: An arrow merely indicates the possibility of a causal connection, while a missing arrow makes a strong empirical claim of zero influence, and a missing double arrow claims zero covariance.
principles.source_quote: It is important to note that, in path diagrams, causal assumptions are encoded not in the links but, rather, in the missing links.

principles.statement: Any mathematical approach to causal analysis must acquire new notation beyond standard probability calculus.
principles.rationale: Standard probability syntax is symmetrical and cannot distinguish between statistical dependence (association) and causal dependence, making it impossible to express directional facts like 'symptoms do not cause diseases.'
principles.source_quote: Another ramification of the sharp distinction between associational and causal concepts is that any mathematical approach to causal analysis must acquire new notation for expressing causal relations – probability calculus is insufficient.

frameworks.name: Graphical Causal Analysis Procedure
frameworks.when_to_apply: When determining if and how a target causal quantity can be estimated from data and assumptions.
steps: Communicate causal assumptions formally using diagrams.
Inspect the diagram to decide whether the assumptions made are sufficient for obtaining consistent estimates of the target quantity.
If affirmative, derive a closed-form expression for the target quantity in terms of distributions of observed quantities.
If negative, suggest a set of observations and experiments that, if performed, would render a consistent estimate feasible.
frameworks.source_quote: The tools described in this section permit investigators to communicate causal assumptions formally using diagrams, then inspect the diagram and 1. Decide whether the assumptions made are sufficient...

frameworks.name: The do(x) Operator (Intervention Simulation)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When modeling the effect of physical interventions or treatments in structural equations to compute post-intervention distributions.
steps: Delete the structural equations (functions) corresponding to the intervened variables from the model.
Replace those deleted functions with a constant X = x.
Keep the rest of the model unchanged to compute the new post-intervention distribution.
frameworks.source_quote: This is done through a mathematical operator called do(x) which simulates physical interventions by deleting certain functions from the model, replacing them by a constant X = x, while keeping the rest of the model unchanged.

mental_models.name: The Demarcation Line (Associational vs. Causal)
mental_models.description: A conceptual boundary separating associational concepts (definable entirely in terms of a joint distribution of observed variables) from causal concepts (cannot be defined from the distribution alone).
mental_models.example: Correlation, regression, and propensity scores are associational concepts. Randomization, confounding, and instrumental variables are causal concepts.

mental_models.name: d-separation
mental_models.description: A graphical criterion used to read off conditional independencies from a causal diagram, allowing the untested assumptions embodied in structural models to confront the scrutiny of nonexperimental data.
mental_models.example: In a chain model UZ -> Z -> X -> Y, the path from UZ to Y is blocked by Z, meaning UX and Y are independent given Z.

heuristics: Correlation does not imply causation translates to: behind every causal conclusion there must lie some causal assumption.
An associational concept is any relationship definable by a joint distribution; a causal concept cannot be defined from the distribution alone.
In path diagrams, causal assumptions are encoded in the missing links.

signature_quotes.text: one cannot substantiate causal claims from associations alone, even at the population level—behind every causal conclusion there must lie some causal assumption that is not testable in observational studies.
signature_quotes.context: Formulating the basic distinction between association and causation.

signature_quotes.text: Causal and associational concepts do not mix.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why standard statistical analysis cannot infer the dynamics of beliefs under changing conditions.

signature_quotes.text: It is important to note that, in path diagrams, causal assumptions are encoded not in the links but, rather, in the missing links.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining how to read and interpret Wright's path diagrams.

signature_quotes.text: The syntax of probability calculus does not permit us to express the simple fact that “symptoms do not cause diseases,” let alone draw mathematical conclusions from such facts.
signature_quotes.context: Highlighting the need for new mathematical notation in causal analysis.

signature_quotes.text: Statisticians can no longer ignore the mental representation in which scientists store experiential knowledge, since it is this representation, and the language used to access it that determine the reliability of the judgments upon which the analysis so crucially depends.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the mental barriers to accepting causal analysis among statisticians.

anti_patterns.description: Defining 'confounding' in purely associational or frequentist statistical terms (e.g., 'U is a potential confounder when both U and X and U and Y are not independent').
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: If confounding could be defined via statistical associations, we could identify and correct for it using nonexperimental data alone, which violates the fundamental rule that causal conclusions require causal assumptions.

anti_patterns.description: Relying solely on standard probability calculus to express causal relationships.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Probability calculus is symmetrical and lacks the vocabulary to distinguish statistical dependence from causal dependence, making it impossible to mathematically express directional facts.
